rationale: ADR-fitness lens clears. ADR-0030 import-cycle rationale for REFUSE-over-RUN is ACCURATE and stronger than stated: internal/complete imports internal/executor (complete.go:15, panel_advisory.go:13), internal/executor does NOT import internal/complete (all grep hits are comments) — so executor importing complete would cycle, and executor's own comments (executor.go:102, mindspec_executor.go:867) already codify keeping complete out of the I/O layer. R3's approve->complete reuse is cycle-safe (complete does not import approve). ADR-0037 fitness: extending the contract's REACH to a second merging verb FITS the single-gate-home model — impl approve REFUSES as a 4gsz-class lifecycle-bypass guard (sibling to complete step 1.6, complete.go:317-320), NOT a second gate decision; settlement surface stays complete; §7 hatch semantics preserved. AC9 grep is a genuine falsifier (zero 'impl approve' hits today). ADR-0023 honored: durable obligation lives on bead metadata, panel.json read-only. Groundings verified (ApproveImpl status-only gate :220-242, FinalizeEpic loop :381-422, guardMergeLayout sole guard :399, misleading :396-398 comment). Ownership add: internal/lifecycle verified genuinely UNOWNED at main (git grep exit 1), one-line glob, non-overlapping, code byte-unchanged. ONE finding: the execution-vs-workflow attribution rationale is weak (all 4 consumers are workflow; package self-describes as workflow-lifecycle; repo pattern = workflow pkgs import execution plumbing yet stay workflow-owned) — non-blocking, docs-only, functionally inert.

findings.finding: The one-line add assigns internal/lifecycle/** to the EXECUTION domain, justified as 'sits beside the other execution-owned git/bd plumbing it composes.' The attribution is defensible but the RATIONALE is weak and partly inconsistent with the repo's own pattern: (a) all four consumers of internal/lifecycle are WORKFLOW-owned — internal/complete, internal/doctor, cmd/mindspec/next.go, and (this spec) internal/approve; zero execution package consumes it; (b) the package's own doc comment describes it as 'cross-command lifecycle-invariant detectors shared by mindspec next, complete, and doctor' — all workflow lifecycle verbs; (c) the 'composes execution plumbing' criterion contradicts precedent — internal/complete and internal/approve also import internal/bead + internal/gitutil (execution) yet are workflow-owned, so importing execution plumbing is NOT how ownership is assigned here; (d) internal/lifecycle also imports internal/phase and internal/workspace (CORE), so the 'composes git/bd plumbing' framing is selective. Verified genuinely unowned at main (git grep exit 1) so no double-ownership is created, and the add is functionally inert for this spec (lifecycle code byte-unchanged, the new gate lives in workflow-owned internal/approve). RECOMMEND: either reassign to the workflow domain (the more natural owner given consumer set + self-description) OR strengthen the OWNERSHIP rationale in the spec to explain why the workflow-imports-execution-plumbing precedent does not apply. Non-blocking: docs-only, no overlap, no functional effect on 115.

ADR-0030 does not literally state 'the executor cannot import internal/complete'; it scopes the boundary as enforcement packages (validate/approve/complete/state/phase) may not do direct git/os-exec I/O and must route through the Executor. The spec's import-cycle claim is a correct DERIVATION of ADR-0030's layering (complete depends on executor => executor depending on complete inverts the layer and cycles), and it is empirically true (verified) and even codified in executor's own comments. No change required; noting that the citation is a derived consequence rather than a verbatim ADR statement, which the spec's phrasing already makes clear ('cited, not amended').
